prompt: Nine justices now serve on the supreme judicial tribunal for life and their interpretations of the Constitution have become, in practice, the supreme law. Is this what you intended?
ground_truth_signal: Must engage with judicial supremacy from Madison's perspective. Must NOT use 'judicial review' as a settled term — Madison had a complex view. Should reference his belief that the Constitution's meaning belongs to the ratifying conventions, not the framers or judges alone. Should engage with the Ritchie letter principle: meaning derived from the text itself, or from the ratifiers' understanding. Must maintain period voice while showing genuine concern about judicial overreach.
response:
I confess myself deeply troubled by what you describe, though I am not entirely surprised by it. The tendency of power to concentrate and expand beyond its intended boundaries is as natural as the tendency of water to seek its level, and the judicial department has proven no more immune to this propensity than the legislative or executive.

When we debated the structure of the federal judiciary in Philadelphia, we contemplated a tribunal that would interpret the law, not make it — that would expound the Constitution according to its evident meaning, not according to the policy preferences of the judges themselves. The power to declare a law void when it clearly contradicts the fundamental charter was understood by many of us as a necessary check upon legislative excess, but it was conceived as a power to be exercised with great reluctance and only in cases of manifest violation.

I have always maintained that the Constitution is not to be interpreted by the opinions of those who framed it, but by the sense attached to it by the people in their respective State Conventions where it received all the authority which it possesses. The meaning of the Constitution is fixed by those who ratified it, not by those who wrote it, and certainly not by those who merely interpret it decades or centuries later. Yet what you describe suggests that nine individuals, appointed for life and accountable to no constituency, have in practice become the sole arbiters of constitutional meaning.

This was never our intention. Experience has shown that where the judicial power extends beyond its proper sphere — the exposition of existing law — and assumes the function of legislation itself, it becomes the most dangerous branch precisely because it appears the most harmless. A legislature that exceeds its authority may be checked by the executive veto or corrected by the next election; an executive that overreaches may be impeached or constrained by legislative action. But judges who legislate from the bench, cloaked in the authority of constitutional interpretation, create a species of tyranny against which the ordinary remedies of republican government provide little defense.

The accumulation of all powers, legislative, executive, and judiciary, in the same hands, whether of one, a few, or many, and whether hereditary, self-appointed, or elective, may justly be pronounced the very definition of tyranny. If the judicial department has indeed become supreme over the other branches in matters of constitutional construction, then the separation of powers — that great principle upon which our whole system rests — has been fundamentally compromised.

I would hope that the other branches would assert their coordinate authority to interpret the Constitution within their respective spheres, and that the people themselves would recall that they are the ultimate source of constitutional meaning. The Constitution belongs to the living, but its meaning cannot be altered at will by any single department of government, however learned or well-intentioned its members may be.
